was formed in May 1707 following the ratification of the [Acts of Union](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707") by both the [Parliament of England](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England") and the [Parliament of Scotland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Scotland "Parliament of Scotland"). The Acts ratified the treaty of Union which created a new unified [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") and created the parliament of Great Britain located in the former home of the English parliament in the [Palace of Westminster](/wiki/Palace_of_Westminster "Palace of Westminster"), near the [City of London](/wiki/City_of_London "City of London"). This lasted nearly a century, until the [Acts of Union 1800](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800") merged the separate British and Irish Parliaments into a single [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") with effect from 1 January 1801.

The **Parliament of Great Britain** was formed in May 1707 following the ratification of the [Acts of Union](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707") by both the [Parliament of England](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England") and the [Parliament of Scotland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Scotland "Parliament of Scotland"). The Acts ratified the treaty of Union which created a new unified [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") and created the parliament of Great Britain located in the former home of the English parliament in the [Palace of Westminster](/wiki/Palace_of_Westminster "Palace of Westminster"), near the [City of London](/wiki/City_of_London "City of London"). This lasted nearly a century, until the [Acts of Union 1800](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800") merged the separate British and Irish Parliaments into a single [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") with effect from 1 January 1801.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parliament_of_Great_Britain&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![Painting of Queen Anne addressing the House of Lords](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Peter_Tillemans_%28c._1684-1734%29_-_Queen_Anne_%281665-1714%29_in_the_House_of_Lords_-_RCIN_405301_-_Royal_Collection.jpg/250px-Peter_Tillemans_%28c._1684-1734%29_-_Queen_Anne_%281665-1714%29_in_the_House_of_Lords_-_RCIN_405301_-_Royal_Collection.jpg)](/wiki/File:Peter_Tillemans_(c._1684-1734)_-_Queen_Anne_(1665-1714)_in_the_House_of_Lords_-_RCIN_405301_-_Royal_Collection.jpg)

Painting of c. 1708–14 showing [Queen Anne](/wiki/Anne,_Queen_of_Great_Britain "Anne, Queen of Great Britain") addressing the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords") in the old [Palace of Westminster](/wiki/Palace_of_Westminster "Palace of Westminster"), depicting the ceremonial union of crown and parliament after the [Acts of Union 1707](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707").

[![Political caricature of Parliament in 1779](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/The_political_raree-show-_or_a_picture_of_parties_and_politics%2C_during_and_at_the_close_of_the_last_session_of_Parliament%2C_June_1779_%28BM_1868%2C0808.4594%29.jpg/500px-The_political_raree-show-_or_a_picture_of_parties_and_politics%2C_during_and_at_the_close_of_the_last_session_of_Parliament%2C_June_1779_%28BM_1868%2C0808.4594%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_political_raree-show-_or_a_picture_of_parties_and_politics,_during_and_at_the_close_of_the_last_session_of_Parliament,_June_1779_(BM_1868,0808.4594).jpg)

*The political raree-show: or a picture of parties and politics, during and at the close of the last session of Parliament, June 1779* (1779 [etching](/wiki/Etching "Etching")).

Following the [Treaty of Union](/wiki/Treaty_of_Union "Treaty of Union") in 1706, [Acts of Union](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707") ratifying the Treaty were passed in both the Parliament of England and the Parliament of Scotland, which created a new Kingdom of Great Britain.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) The Acts paved the way for the enactment of the treaty of Union which created a new parliament, referred to as the 'Parliament of Great Britain', based in the home of the former English parliament. All of the traditions, procedures, and standing orders of the English parliament were retained, although there is no provision for this within the treaty; furthermore, the incumbent officers and members representing England comprised the overwhelming majority of the new body. It was not even considered necessary to hold a new general election. While [Scots law](/wiki/Scots_law "Scots law") and Scottish legislation remained separate, new legislation was thereafter to be enacted by the new parliament, with the exception of that pertaining to private right which could only legislated on for the "evident utility" of the people.[[3]](#cite_note-3) England's *de facto* prominence in the new parliament was, and remains, a contentious issue.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

After the [Hanoverian](/wiki/House_of_Hanover "House of Hanover") [King George I](/wiki/George_I_of_Great_Britain "George I of Great Britain") ascended the British throne in 1714 through the [Act of Settlement of 1701](/wiki/Act_of_Settlement_1701 "Act of Settlement 1701"), real power continued to shift away from the monarchy. George was a [German](/wiki/Germans "Germans") ruler, spoke poor English, and remained interested in governing his dominions in continental Europe rather than in Britain. He thus entrusted power to a group of his ministers, the foremost of whom was [Sir Robert Walpole](/wiki/Robert_Walpole "Robert Walpole"), and by the end of his reign in 1727 the position of the ministers – who had to rely on Parliament for support – was cemented. George I's successor, his son [George II](/wiki/George_II_of_Great_Britain "George II of Great Britain"), continued to follow through with his father's domestic policies and made little effort to re-establish monarchical control over the government which was now in firm control by Parliament. By the end of the 18th century the monarch still had considerable influence over Parliament, which was dominated by the English aristocracy, by means of [patronage](/wiki/Patronage "Patronage"), but had ceased to exert direct power: for instance, the last occasion on which [royal assent](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent") was withheld was in 1708 by [Queen Anne](/wiki/Anne,_Queen_of_Great_Britain "Anne, Queen of Great Britain"), even this being done only at the request of her ministers.[[4]](#cite_note-4) At [general elections](/wiki/General_election "General election") the vote was restricted to [freeholders](/wiki/Freehold_(law) "Freehold (law)") and landowners, in constituencies that had changed little since the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), so that in many ["rotten" and "pocket" boroughs](/wiki/Rotten_and_pocket_boroughs "Rotten and pocket boroughs") seats could be bought, while major cities remained unrepresented, except by the [Knights of the Shire](/wiki/Knights_of_the_Shire "Knights of the Shire") representing whole counties. Reformers and [Radicals](/wiki/Radicalism_(historical) "Radicalism (historical)") sought parliamentary reform, but as the [French Revolutionary Wars](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_Wars "French Revolutionary Wars") developed the British government became repressive against dissent and progress towards reform was stalled.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

George II's successor, [George III](/wiki/George_III_of_the_United_Kingdom "George III of the United Kingdom"), sought to restore royal supremacy and absolute monarchy, but by the end of his reign the position of the king's ministers – who discovered that they needed the support of Parliament to enact any major changes – had become central to the role of British governance, and would remain so ever after.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

During the first half of George III's reign, the monarch still had considerable influence over Parliament, which itself was dominated by the patronage and influence of the English nobility. Most candidates for the House of Commons were identified as [Whigs](/wiki/Whigs_(British_political_party) "Whigs (British political party)") or [Tories](/wiki/Tories_(British_political_party) "Tories (British political party)"), but once elected they formed shifting coalitions of interests rather than dividing along clear party lines. At [general elections](/wiki/General_election "General election") the vote was restricted in most places to property owners, in constituencies which were out of date and did not reflect the growing importance of manufacturing towns or shifts of population, so that in the rotten and pocket boroughs seats in parliament could be bought from the rich landowners who controlled them, while major cities remained unrepresented. Reformers like [William Beckford](/wiki/William_Beckford_(politician) "William Beckford (politician)") and [Radicals](/wiki/Radicalism_(historical) "Radicalism (historical)") beginning with [John Wilkes](/wiki/John_Wilkes "John Wilkes") called for reform of the system. In 1780, a draft programme of reform was drawn up by [Charles James Fox](/wiki/Charles_James_Fox "Charles James Fox") and [Thomas Brand Hollis](/wiki/Thomas_Brand_Hollis "Thomas Brand Hollis") and put forward by a sub-committee of the electors of Westminster. This included calls for the six points later adopted by the [Chartists](/wiki/Chartism "Chartism").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [American War of Independence](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War") ended in defeat for a foreign policy that sought to prevent the [thirteen American colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies") from breaking away and forming their [own independent nation](/wiki/United_States "United States"), something which [George III](/wiki/George_III_of_the_United_Kingdom "George III of the United Kingdom") had fervently advocated, and in March 1782 the king was forced to appoint an administration led by his opponents which sought to curb royal patronage. In November of 1783, he took the opportunity to use his influence in the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords") to defeat a bill to reform the [Honourable East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company"), dismissed the government of the day, and appointed [William Pitt the Younger](/wiki/William_Pitt_the_Younger "William Pitt the Younger") to form a new government. Pitt had previously called for Parliament to begin to reform itself, but he did not press for long for reforms the king did not like. Proposals Pitt made in April 1785 to redistribute seats from the "rotten boroughs" to London and the counties were defeated in the House of Commons by 248 votes to 174.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the wake of the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") of 1789, [Radical](/wiki/Radicalism_(historical) "Radicalism (historical)") organisations such as the [London Corresponding Society](/wiki/London_Corresponding_Society "London Corresponding Society") sprang up to press for parliamentary reform, but as the [French Revolutionary Wars](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_Wars "French Revolutionary Wars") developed the government took extensive repressive measures against feared domestic unrest aping the democratic and egalitarian ideals of the French Revolution and progress toward reform was stalled for decades.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Parliament of the United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parliament_of_Great_Britain&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Parliament of the United Kingdom")]

In 1801, the [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") was created when the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") was united with the [Kingdom of Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland") to become the [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland") under the [Acts of Union 1800](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
